title: Functional interfaces and lambdas

canonicalExplanation.summary: A lambda supplies behavior for a functional interface, receives target typing from context, and may capture only final or effectively final local bindings.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Comparator<String> lambda compares length then text, and a method reference replaces it only when the same contract remains clear. Your goal is to write and trace lambdas while avoiding mutable captured-state surprises. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration, mutation and immutability; Interfaces. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A lambda supplies behavior for a functional interface, receives target typing from context, and may capture only final or effectively final local bindings. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Captured object state can still mutate, this refers to the enclosing instance, overloads can make target typing ambiguous, and side effects complicate reuse or parallel execution. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Effectively final capture makes every referenced object immutable and gives each lambda a private copy of its state.

includes: Implement single-abstract-method behavior with method references and captured values.
Write and trace lambdas while avoiding mutable captured-state surprises.
excludes: Captured object state can still mutate, this refers to the enclosing instance, overloads can make target typing ambiguous, and side effects complicate reuse or parallel execution.

outcomes: Write and trace lambdas while avoiding mutable captured-state surprises.

examples.title: Functional interfaces and lambdas: direct language case
examples.situation: A Comparator<String> lambda compares length then text, and a method reference replaces it only when the same contract remains clear.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A Comparator<String> lambda compares length then text, and a method reference replaces it only when the same contract remains clear.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Capture copies of local binding values under effectively final rules; referenced mutable objects remain shared, so make side effects explicit or avoid them.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Capture copies of local binding values under effectively final rules; referenced mutable objects remain shared, so make side effects explicit or avoid them.

examples.title: Functional interfaces and lambdas: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A lambda captures an effectively final threshold but accumulates results through an explicit collector instead of mutating an external list.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A lambda captures an effectively final threshold but accumulates results through an explicit collector instead of mutating an external list.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Captured object state can still mutate, this refers to the enclosing instance, overloads can make target typing ambiguous, and side effects complicate reuse or parallel execution.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Effectively final capture makes every referenced object immutable and gives each lambda a private copy of its state.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write and trace lambdas while avoiding mutable captured-state surprises.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Effectively final capture makes every referenced object immutable and gives each lambda a private copy of its state.
misconceptions.correction: Capture copies of local binding values under effectively final rules; referenced mutable objects remain shared, so make side effects explicit or avoid them.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Effectively final capture makes every referenced object immutable and gives each lambda a private copy of its state.

recap.summary: You should now be able to write and trace lambdas while avoiding mutable captured-state surprises. Keep this boundary in view: Captured object state can still mutate, this refers to the enclosing instance, overloads can make target typing ambiguous, and side effects complicate reuse or parallel execution.
